front: Empire: shares its name with the modern country of Mali, whose capital is Bamako
work: General / Giveaway Identifiers

front: Empire: its founding is recounted in the oral Epic of Sundiata, preserved and performed by griots
work: General / Giveaway Identifiers

front: Ruler: legendarily disabled, not walking until age seven, fulfilling a prophecy that he would marry the hunchbacked Sogolon
work: Founding: Sundiata Keita & the Battle of Kirina

front: Battle: Sundiata Keita's forces defeated the Sosso king Sumanguru Kante here, founding the Mali Empire
work: Founding: Sundiata Keita & the Battle of Kirina

front: Event: in one retelling of this battle, Sumanguru Kante was wounded by an arrow tipped with a white rooster's spur after a taunting exchange of boasts
work: Founding: Sundiata Keita & the Battle of Kirina

front: Event: after the Battle of Kirina, the allied Mandinka towns became known as this empire's "Twelve Doors"
work: Founding: Sundiata Keita & the Battle of Kirina

front: Event: conquered the older Empire of Ghana in 1235
work: Founding: Sundiata Keita & the Battle of Kirina

front: Document: 44-article constitution covering social organization, goods, environmental preservation, and funerary customs, adopted after the Battle of Kirina
work: Government: The Gbara Assembly & the Kouroukan Fouga

front: Institution: 32-member deliberative "Great Assembly" whose first meeting brought together 29 clans under a belen-tigui
work: Government: The Gbara Assembly & the Kouroukan Fouga

front: Event: sixteen "quiver-carrier" clans were responsible for this empire's military defense
work: Government: The Gbara Assembly & the Kouroukan Fouga

front: Event: seven nyamakala clans were believed to possess a creative force enabling their crafts and art forms
work: Government: The Gbara Assembly & the Kouroukan Fouga

front: Event: territorial governors were called farbas
work: Government: The Gbara Assembly & the Kouroukan Fouga

front: Ruler: his lavish hajj to Mecca devalued the price of gold across Egypt
work: Mansa Musa & the Pilgrimage to Mecca

front: Ruler: commissioned the Djinguereber Mosque and the mudbrick learning center at Sankore in Timbuktu
work: Mansa Musa & the Pilgrimage to Mecca

front: Architect: this Granadan Sufi poet is traditionally credited with building the Djinguereber Mosque and Sankore for Mansa Musa
work: Mansa Musa & the Pilgrimage to Mecca

front: Ruler: depicted in the Catalan Atlas holding a fleur-de-lis staff in one hand and a gold nugget in the other
work: Mansa Musa & the Pilgrimage to Mecca

front: Ruler: reportedly brought as many as 60,000 men with him on his hajj to Mecca
work: Mansa Musa & the Pilgrimage to Mecca

front: Ruler: this formerly enslaved freedman usurped the throne from the founder's grandson as the empire's fifth ruler
work: Later Mansas & Succession Crises

front: Ruler: seized the copper-producing Takedda region
work: Later Mansas & Succession Crises

front: Ruler: one mansa of this empire gave a traveler only bread, fried meat, and sour milk, and made his court poets wear bird masks
work: Later Mansas & Succession Crises

front: Work: this chronicle follows a mansa of the Mali Empire who accidentally killed his own mother
work: Later Mansas & Succession Crises

front: Event: one mansa of this empire is said to have vanished into a "great whirlpool" at sea on an exploratory voyage before the husband of Inare Kunati took the throne
work: Later Mansas & Succession Crises

front: Event: the traveler Ibn Battuta praised local children's diligence in memorizing the Qur'an here but complained that slaves and women went about topless
work: Society & Economy

front: Event: Ibn Battuta observed the mansas' chief interpreter, the jeli, a precursor to the later griots
work: Society & Economy

front: Event: by its later years, was ruled primarily by the Bambara and Mandinka ethnic groups
work: Society & Economy

front: Event: prospered as a hub of the trans-Saharan salt-and-gold trade
work: Society & Economy

front: Place: early capital of the Mali Empire
work: Cities & Landmarks

front: Place: became the Mali Empire's major cultural hub, reached via the trading city of Djenne
work: Cities & Landmarks

front: Battle: the Mali Empire lost this battle to Morocco
work: Decline & Aftermath

front: Event: under final ruler Mahmud IV, the Tarikh al-Sudan records that this empire's troops, split between the sanqara-zuma and farim-soura divisions, could not save him in its final battle
work: Decline & Aftermath

front: Event: this successor empire was established following a revolt against Mali, and its cities came to include Timbuktu
work: Decline & Aftermath

front: Work: in this oral epic, the griot Balla Fasseke sings the "Hymn to the Bow" after Sundiata bends an iron bar while learning to stand
work: Legacy: The Epic of Sundiata & Modern Depictions

front: Work: this oral epic was translated into English by D.T. Niane
work: Legacy: The Epic of Sundiata & Modern Depictions

front: Author: studied the oral tradition behind the Epic of Sundiata, including the fictional griot Balla Fasseke
work: Legacy: The Epic of Sundiata & Modern Depictions

front: Novel: follows Raymond Spartacus Kassoumi, who returns to the fictional nation of Nakem-Zuiko as it seeks independence
work: Legacy: The Epic of Sundiata & Modern Depictions

front: Novel: was accused of plagiarizing Graham Greene
